name: Pazuzu
description: Pazuzu is a Mesopotamian demon best known as the king of the demons of the wind and the son of the god Hanbi. He is depicted with a fearsome appearance, combining human and animal features. He has the body of a man, the head of a lion or dog, talons of an eagle, two pairs of wings, and a scorpion's tail. Despite his demonic nature, Pazuzu is primarily considered a protective deity, warding off evil winds and disease, particularly those carried by his rival, the demon Lamashtu, who threatened pregnant women and infants. Amulets and statues of Pazuzu were commonly used to protect against her malevolent influence. While capable of causing harm, Pazuzu's power was often invoked for benevolent purposes, making him a complex figure in Mesopotamian demonology, embodying both destructive and protective forces. He represents the chaotic nature of the wind and the power to control it, a vital aspect of life in the ancient Near East. The use of Pazuzu imagery demonstrates a belief in the ability to harness even the most terrifying forces for good, highlighting the nuanced understanding of good and evil in Mesopotamian beliefs. His image and name were commonly inscribed on amulets and used in exorcism rituals, solidifying his role as a protector against demonic forces.
subtitle: King of the Wind Demons
appearance: Pazuzu is a composite being with the body of a man, the head of a lion or dog, eagle-like talons, two pairs of wings, and a scorpion's tail. His face is often grotesque, with bulging eyes and a snarling mouth. He is typically depicted standing or kneeling, often with his right hand raised and his left hand lowered.
habitat: Pazuzu is associated with the winds and the open sky, but his influence extends to any place where evil spirits might reside, particularly homes and vulnerable individuals.
behavior: While a demon, Pazuzu primarily acts as a protector, warding off evil spirits, especially Lamashtu. He can be invoked to control the winds and prevent them from bringing disease. He is a powerful and fearsome figure, but his primary function is to defend against greater evils.
weaknesses: Specific incantations and rituals can bind him

keyMyths.title: Pazuzu vs. Lamashtu
keyMyths.description: Pazuzu is the sworn enemy of Lamashtu, a female demon who preys on pregnant women and infants. Amulets of Pazuzu were used to protect against Lamashtu's attacks, as it was believed that Pazuzu's power could overcome hers. He is often depicted in opposition to her, actively trying to thwart her evil deeds.

sources.author: Jeremy Black and Anthony Green
sources.work: Gods, Demons and Symbols of Ancient Mesopotamia
sources.description: Comprehensive overview of Mesopotamian mythology and iconography, including detailed information on Pazuzu.
sources.text: Comprehensive overview of Mesopotamian mythology and iconography, including detailed information on Pazuzu.

sources.author: Henri Frankfort
sources.work: Cylinder Seals: A Documentary Essay on the Art and Religion of the Ancient Near East
sources.description: Discusses the representation of Pazuzu on cylinder seals and its significance in Mesopotamian religious beliefs.
sources.text: Discusses the representation of Pazuzu on cylinder seals and its significance in Mesopotamian religious beliefs.
